title: Shape Fluctuations of Growing Droplets and Random Matrix Theory
speaker: Herbert Spohn
abstract: We explain an exact solution of the one-dimensional Kardar-Parisi-Zhang equation with sharp wedge initial data. Physically this solution describes the shape fluctuations of a thin film droplet formed by the stable phase expanding into the unstable phase. In the long time limit our solution converges to the Tracy-Widom distribution of the largest eigenvalue of GUE random matrices.

proof_stages.label: One-dimensional moving-interface universality class
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames growing one-dimensional fronts separating stable and metastable phases and identifies flat and curved/droplet geometries across several lattice and continuum growth models.

proof_stages.label: KPZ equation and scaling prediction
proof_stages.summary: The KPZ stochastic PDE is introduced as a continuum model for graph-like interfaces, with the nonlinear slope term motivated geometrically and the one-point scaling form involving model-dependent coefficients and a universal t^(1/3) random amplitude.

proof_stages.label: Experimental Tracy-Widom test
proof_stages.summary: The Takeuchi-Sano liquid-crystal experiment realizes point- and line-seeded growth, and after determining the nonuniversal scales its fluctuation distributions match GUE and GOE Tracy-Widom laws with observable t^(-1/3) finite-time shifts.

proof_stages.label: Sharp-wedge KPZ droplet specialization
proof_stages.summary: The analysis is specialized to KPZ with sharp-wedge initial data, whose deterministic part becomes a parabolic droplet profile while the centered height is expected to have t^(1/3) GUE Tracy-Widom one-point fluctuations and Airy/Dyson spatial correlations.

proof_stages.label: Cole-Hopf definition of the ill-posed SPDE
proof_stages.summary: Because the nonlinear KPZ equation is not directly well posed, the height is defined as the logarithm of the stochastic heat equation fundamental solution, equivalently a point-to-point continuum directed-polymer partition function made rigorous through Ito/chaos expansion.

proof_stages.label: Fredholm determinant formula and Airy limit
proof_stages.summary: For the Cole-Hopf sharp-wedge solution, an exact generating function is expressed as a Fredholm determinant with an explicit Airy-type kernel, and long-time rescaling turns this kernel into the Airy kernel, yielding the GUE Tracy-Widom distribution.

proof_stages.label: Weak-driving and high-temperature routes to KPZ
proof_stages.summary: Weakly asymmetric exclusion and weak-noise discrete directed polymers are presented as scaling limits in which KPZ becomes the first nontrivial high-temperature/weak-driving limit while retaining universal fluctuation information.

proof_stages.label: Finite-time corrections and further solvable models
proof_stages.summary: Exact and numerical comparisons show that convergence to Tracy-Widom can have model-dependent finite-time corrections, and the talk closes with open flat-initial-data questions and links to semi-discrete polymers, Dyson Brownian motion, and quantum Toda/Whittaker structures.

examples.name: Low-temperature Ising/Glauber moving interface
examples.note: The two-dimensional Ising interface under a small magnetic field makes concrete the stable/metastable phase setup and the flat-versus-droplet geometries, but it is not used in the later KPZ proof.
examples.engagement_reason: He traces the low-temperature Ising/Glauber interface dynamics, showing which spins flip, how the staircase moves, and how it maps after rotation to particles and holes.

examples.note: By comparing vertical velocities of flat and tilted pieces of an isotropically expanding front, the example produces the geometric reason for the quadratic KPZ nonlinearity.
examples.engagement_reason: He derives the KPZ nonlinear slope term from isotropic normal growth, computing the sloped-front vertical velocity and Taylor expanding it.

examples.name: Random-matrix edge normalization
examples.note: In response to a question, the semicircle-law edge picture explains why Tracy-Widom fluctuations are measured relative to the asymptotic edge and need not have zero mean.
examples.engagement_reason: He works with the random-matrix edge scaling, locating the semicircle edge and explaining the largest-eigenvalue normalization by an n^{1/3} Tracy-Widom fluctuation.

examples.name: TASEP/Johansson versus KPZ finite-time approach
examples.note: The comparison between a totally asymmetric lattice growth simulation and numerical KPZ distributions is used to draw the lesson that the limiting law is universal while finite-time corrections are model-dependent.
examples.engagement_reason: He compares the Johansson/TASEP finite-time distribution with the KPZ exact-solution distribution, tracking how each approaches the Tracy-Widom limit from different sides.

passes.reason: TASEP is the totally asymmetric boundary case and is contrasted with KPZ to show finite-time corrections are not universal.
